nodes.text: Sam has been awake since five-fifty-two. She is at her desk now, in her pajamas, with the lamp on against the grey of the storm. She has in front of her a notebook she bought at the Kwik Stop last night on her way home — one of the small cheap spiral-bound ones they stock for high-school kids, ninety-nine cents, lined pages, stapled on the short edge. She has not, until this summer, been a person who kept a notebook.
She is making a list. The list has a small header at the top in block capitals: WEDNESDAY LIST.
1. Do not call dad during the day unless he calls first. If he calls first, answer on the third ring.
2. Do not go to the Gas & Go. Do not drive past the Gas & Go. Take the alternate route to Cosmic Comics if I go there.
3. Do not contact Maya during her shift unless she contacts me first.
4. If sedan shows up at Kwik Stop during my shift, do not acknowledge.
5. Eat lunch. Do not skip it.
6. If dad asks about last night's thunder, say I slept through it.
7. Remember that whatever F.T. is, I am, right now, being watched by someone who wants to help. It is okay to let myself be watched.
8. Write down everything dad says today. Every sentence. Even the boring ones. Patterns are in the boring.
She looks at the list. She crosses out item 3. She rewrites it: If Maya contacts me, answer in under five minutes. If Maya does not contact me, do not worry — she is working.
She looks at the list again. She adds item 9: Breathe.
She adds item 10: It is Wednesday. Diego has been missing for four days. I am going to find him. I do not need to find him today.
She closes the notebook. She slides it between the mattress and the box spring.

She stands. She goes to the window. The rain is hitting the window hard. The cul-de-sac below is running with a thin sheet of water that is not, quite, flooding — the grid's quiet efficiency holding, as advertised — but that is, for the first time in Sam's memory, visible as a sheet, continuous, catching the overhead streetlight in a long rippling silver that extends across all three driveways opposite and down toward the storm drain at the bend.

In the silver, for a single instant, she sees — she thinks she sees — a reflection that is not the lamp in her own window. Not her window at all. A different window. A different lamp. A different face.

She blinks. The face is gone. It had been, she realizes with a small slow settling-in, a face she half-recognized. An older woman. Dark hair going silver. Kind eyes. A face that was not, in any way Sam could articulate, frightening.
That was my grandmother.
Her grandmother on her mother's side had died in 2019.
Sam stands at the window for another forty-five seconds. The face does not reappear. The rain keeps coming down. She turns away. She goes to the closet. She gets dressed for work.
